speaker: interviewer
text: Up until the time of the liquidation of the ghetto, you were still working as a wood turner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was working in-- in the [GERMAN] in the workshops in the ghetto until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the last actually day when they start to take us to that gate to transport to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any extraordinary experiences with your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I had a lot of experiences while I was working in the [GERMAN]. As I recall, in 1941 when the [GERMAN], I think it was around December [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] before Christmas, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] a-- the German commandant from the [GERMAN] and the ghetto-- Commandant Wiedmann was his name. Wiedmann. W-I-E-D-M-A-N-N. He was the commandant, and taking care and checking out how the workshops work, and now everybody's manufacturing things, and how all the work is going on, and if everything goes through. Used to come all the time and check out how all the workers are working. And he was very happy all the time, seeing that the leather coats by the tailors are coming out excellent, and the leather boots are the most beautiful work he ever seen. And the German uniforms are made excellent to order for the big gestapo people and the other people. And the toy factory is doing a golden job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, one of his friends [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who had vacation from the Russian front, one of his comrades, he invited to show off. He was the big leader with that swastika, you know, carrying on with the ghetto. He was showing off his friend all the job it's doing with the Juden, with the Jews, and with the work. So he took him on a tour, that German, his friend who came for vacation from front, from the Russian front. And they start walking him to the workshops. The German, his friend, when he came in, he was angry like a lion, like a tiger.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had in his hand a leather strap like the German were, you know, where they beat people. He didn't know who to cut and who to slice over the head, you know, with that strap, the way he was walking through. All the-- the yard in this-- in the-- in the factory like a monster. That how angry he was. And Mr. Wiedmann was just going with him. He didn't say a word, you know. But then he came into my department right from the yard. We all looked through the window. We saw the anger, and we all start to work right by the lathes, you know. Fast. He's coming in, into our shops. And he walked in. We saw the anger on his face. When he looked. And then he walked by my lathe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, I was turning a chess set from little bones. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's called a pocket chess set. It's so small, the figures, that you put them in, you know, with little pins. It's turned on-- and each figure from the chess is turned on a small little pin, and in a little box with a chess set and you stick in and you play like that. And you can keep in your pocket. A beautiful job. I turned the chess set. And he looked at the lathe, the way I turned. And the white shavings from the figures, you know, fall right on my lathe. And the way I shape it. And he looked. And at that time, I was young, and I had very blond hair. I mean, real blond. Now I'm gray, so you would never know. But you'll see the pictures there when I was young. Real blond.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he saw the way I was turning my chess set. He looked again in my face. And he came closer to me. And he said to me, are you a Jew? I said, yes, I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, but your father was not a Jew. I said, my father was Jewish. My mother is Jewish. And my grandfather are Jewish. And we are all wood turners. And in Germany, it's called [GERMAN]. We are all [GERMAN], and I told him in German. [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means, it couldn't be. It couldn't be. The Jewish people can't do that type of work. And while his friend, you know, the commandant was marching around there, not standing even close by him. He was marching on in another direction. That German murderous man that came in, start making conversation with me. He said again, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aren't you making a mistake? I says, no. We are all Jews. He says, how could it be? The Jews know only one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taught by the Fuhrer, and we were taught in our schools, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were shown pictures what the Jewish do. They are dirty, dirty people. And they do only swindle jobs. They are not workers. They don't have no trade. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They are not tradespeople.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how could it be that all these-- your whole family were all wood turners? That means something is wrong somewhere. Said it means, he says, the Fuhrer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] said a lie. And all my leaders are lying. That's how he was talking to me, quietly, right by the lathe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, he says, it couldn't be. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he looked again at me. And I said to him, when you'll go through all the other departments, you'll see shoe makers, tailors, and uniforms, the most beautiful things. And all made by Jews. In Lithuania and in all the Baltic countries, and even in Poland, the best shoemakers and the best tailors and the best cabinet makers and the best carvers and the best wood turners are all Jewish. He says, oh. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He said, this I didn't know. And he took one of my figures what I had on the lathe turned. And he took out a small pen. And he says, if you don't mind, put down your name underneath the figure, write your name. So it was so small, you know, that I had to make small, like very thin writing. I put on Moses. M-O-- all around the figure on the bottom. M-- Moses. And he put it in in his pocket. He says, I'm going to show it to my comrades when I go back to Germany, the beautiful work the Jewish are doing for the German Reich. And I'm going to also tell them, he says, not-- that the Fuhrer said some lies. And he was talking very quietly. And when he left, he threw a pack of cigarettes right under my lathe. And he was no more running around with his leather strap and looking like a murderer. He had such a change in his face that he was not the same man. And I had a couple of times [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] similar cases [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the war time with certain officers like him. Because there's a lot of German SS [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who became so devoted because they never lived in with Jews. They came from-- some-- some of them came from towns where they never saw a Jew. They saw Jewish only the propaganda school when they became SS soldiers. How the Jews looks, like rats, with beards dirty, with all different things. So they--they never met Jews. And then all of a sudden, Lithuania is a country that the Jewish people are different than other countries, like different than Poland. Very few Jews-- my grandfather wore a beard. Other couple of Jews wear beards. But most Lithuanish Jews were smoothly shaved and they didn't have beards, and they didn't wear hats. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The same thing like in the United States, people. Clean shaved, cut, nicely dressed, and without hats. And Germans didn't know that. Some of the Germans, the old Germans, SS, they knew because they lived with Jews and they went in schools and in colleges with Jews. But the young German generation SS, some of the hooligans learned only how a Jew looks. And when they came in and they see clean cut Jews in the ghetto, when we were in the ghetto we were still nicely dressed because we were-- still had our clothes. We were not in striped uniforms. And we were 100%, you know, skeletons. And we still had our hair. We were not shaved like in the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when those Germans saw how Jews look, so they thought these Jews look almost like we look. Understand? So they had-- some of them had already little opinions and little, you know, changes of their thinking. I couldn't, you know, make think the all SS troops like that. I wish I could. Believe me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know if I could change them. But some of them were in a lot of disappointing-- disappointment, seeing what Jewish hands can do. And he took that little chess figure with him right in the pocket. And then he kept on going through further up. I didn't see him anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: To other departments of the workshops. And a lot of the tailors-- my uncle was a tailor. He told me. I says, did a man was a hooligan came in, in your department? He says, no. He was very quiet and was standing still.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, yes. I think I was the first one who calmed him down maybe. So-- I mean, this is a piece of history you can never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: It might have saved some lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they began to liquidate the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what were you thinking? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were thinking that we are going to be killed. We are taken to a place somewheres or in Germany, or somewheres in Lithuania in the outskirts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't know where we were going. But we were from the last group, my family. Before me, in the morning, were taken already a lot of Jews to the train. To the train-- to the railroad. To the railroad station. But we were still, you know, pushing ourselves to be the last. Maybe a miracle will be. So I was in the [GERMAN], actually, till the last maybe 400, 500 Jews there on the place. We were no more on the [GERMAN]. We were taken to the gate. But we were from the 400 or 500, the last Jews. I'll tell you the thing. When they [GERMAN] were closed, a German gestapo truck came in to pick up some laundry in the-- because it was a laundry department. And nobody was there in the [GERMAN] no more. Everybody was already gone, you know, to the transport, to the railroad-- to the railroad. But I was still there with my family in the [GERMAN] yard, because in the [GERMAN] yard was a [YIDDISH]. A [YIDDISH] means there was a cellar dug out by some people who walked in the [GERMAN] during the years, a hiding place. And one of the Jewish police who were all the time in the [GERMAN] gate staying all the time was a very good friend of my Uncle Boris. And he told me-- his name was Yonkel Glick, Jacob Glick. He says, Maishke. Your mother is here, and children. Let's go underneath. There's a [YIDDISH], a bunker in the ground with a certain hiding place to hide. And we'll go under there, and maybe we'll wait out. The Russians, the Soviet army, is not far from Lithuania. In weeks, Lithuania is going to be liberated by the Russians. So we crawled in in that with my family, my mother and my brother and sister, inside. But other people maybe knew about that bunker too, and life is very dear. Everybody wants to stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: So more people with force, I want to go in too. I want to be alive too. So everybody start to press himself in in that bunker. In that [YIDDISH], called in Jewish. It's a [YIDDISH]. The [YIDDISH] was not big enough to take so many people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother and even me, we start to feel like we are going to faint there. If we're not going to die by the Germans guns, we are going to die here in the hole. So my mother says, let's go out. So we went out from the [YIDDISH], and we walked all the way towards that [GERMAN] near the gates where everybody was gathered together for the transport to the railways, to the railroad. And we were from the last people to actually march out from the ghetto by the guards, the German guards, with the machine guns. Taken, marching all the way to the railroad station. But before we marched out from the gates, from that [GERMAN] there, the Germans were burning-- was a two story house. I don't know if it was a medical clinic. Right near the gate, what it was there. Or offices. And they took a man, a Jewish man there. They lit it, that place, in fire. And while it was burning, they took a man. His name was Liptzer. And that Liptzer [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a Jew who worked for two, three years for the gestapo. He worked inside in the offices there. I don't know what type of work he do. If he was, you know, a-- what do you call, a double crosser, you know. A-- a man, you know, he gives out, you know, certain information to the gestapo. We didn't know. Nobody can tell real the truth. But he was working there in the gestapo. If he was just cleaning windows, or he was-- we didn't know. For years, he was working there. But the fact is before liquidating the ghetto, they took Liptzer, that man-- I saw with my own eyes. And they marched him in the burning house. They pushed him in the there, and they burned with the house, the ghetto. With-- not-- with a lot of more other people were there. Sick people, you know. People who couldn't walk on the march. So he walked in in that house there on the left side of the gate. I don't know exactly what exactly it was in that house there. It was administration or something. And the-- while we we were walking, the house was burning. And that's how we were walking all the way to the railroad under heavy guard. And while we were walking under heavy guard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and this time the guard was real heavy. It was no plain rifles. It was machine guns they had. All the green uniform SS or NKVD. I don't know what department of the German army it was. NKVD, I think it was. It's like the SS. The sad situation was while we were taken to the railroad, the German army, the Wehrmacht, was retreating too. They were already being chased by the Soviet Army. We were going to the railroad, and the German army was running for their lives to save themselves, the Wehrmacht. And I've seen, like I told you before, the shiny German army who I thought they were going to take the world with the shiny boots, drinking the champagne, with the uniforms, being kissed by the Lithuanian girls. I've seen the same German army running without boots, crawling, dirty, torn clothes. They were laying on the sidewalks and on the streets. And I heard with my own ears while going through, the officer told one of the German soldiers from the Wehrmacht, in German, get up. Don't forget. You are a German soldier. Shame on you. Up! But the man couldn't get up, the soldier. He had-- his legs were all blown up, torn. He didn't have boots even on his feet. And I said to my mother-- and my mother start crying, too. We are all crying. We are seeing the end. We are seeing the revenge. We are seeing the downfall of the Third Reich. Look how they looking, look at them. And we are marching on to the railroad, to the cattle trains. The German falling armor. They needed the cattle trains to save their life more than we. They didn't give them the-- the cattle trains to save them and run with them back to Germany. They had to walk and fall in the streets, the German army. Running, running, because they were chased-- not to be caught by the Russian-- by the Soviet army. For us, they had trains, the cattle trains. But the Wehrmacht was falling in the street, couldn't walk. Looked like dirt. They looked like the Russian prisoners. When the Germans marched in in 1941, they made the Russian prisoners. To look so beaten. When I looked at the Russian prisoners in 1941, I said, they don't look no more like humans. They were beaten, you know, with the butts from the guns by the German armies. They beat them. They-- they made them dirt, dirty looking. And they marched the Russian army to the main Kaunas streets to show the Lithuanians, the Gentile people, look at what we did with the Russian army. Showing off in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, July the 13th, they were looking worse than the Russian army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, not so hungered out on the faces. But the clothes, the shoes, torn, running. You understand? So I said-- my mother was crying. She says, Kinder, look. We are seeing, she says, the [YIDDISH]. The [YIDDISH]. That means we are seeing the revenge of them. And we are going to be, I'm afraid, killed. And that was the outcry by many-- not only our family, by many families. It was the pleasure to see them running, falling in the street, the German army. And where we are-- where are we going? What's going to be with us? We don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of our friends, after crossing from Slabodke to Kaunas, there is a bridge. Because underneath was the Valea. That's a lake flowing. So was a iron bridge, a small bridge to cross over. A lot of the people-- of the Jewish people jumped over the bridge in the water, maybe to save themselves. But nobody was saved. The German guard was so strong, and they all machine gunned them. And we were all taken to the railroad. And we were all start [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pushed in like cattle, with such a force, with such a murder, anger, to the railroad cattle cars. Not like human beings. Everything was-- that's why I use a lot that word-- that word, chased. We were never-- when it came to these type situations, we were all the time chased. Not walked. One was falling on top of the other. In, in, in the trains. And I told my mother, before we went into the trains-- because I spoke with some of my friends when we left the ghetto. To my friend Yonkel Weitz, Bianke Weitz, his brother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Chayet, another Ringer. And David-- Davidson, a Ringer. Four guys. We made up our mind when we are in the train, if we are going to go by train-- we knew we are going to the railroad. But we-- and if we are going to the certain place we are thinking we are going, we are going to jump the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't want to go in in the same train with my mother and with my brother and with my sister, with my family. In case by jumping I'll be shot, I didn't want my mother to see that. So I went in in another car with my friends, with a lot of other people, you know. And-- OK, let's stop for a minute right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
